How the Web Works: Part 1 of 4
DNS Explained: How the Internet Finds Any Website in Milliseconds
You open websites every day.
You type an address, press Enter, and within seconds a page appears on your screen.
But have you ever wondered what actually happens between those two moments?
This series breaks it all down.
By the end, you won’t just browse the web. you’ll understand how it works.
Press enter or click to view image in full size

How the web works
The Problem DNS Solves
Every device on the internet has an IP address. something like 104.26.10.229 Nobody wants to memorise number strings to visit websites. DNS (Domain Name System) lets you type Google.com and invisibly translates it into the real IP address in milliseconds. Think of DNS like a phone book, you look up a name, it gives you a number.
Press enter or click to view image in full size

phone book pages
Anatomy of a Domain Name
Take admin.google.com as an example:
Press enter or click to view image in full size

Domain name breakdownTLD Top-Level Domain
The rightmost part of a domain. Two types: gTLD (generic: .com, .org, .edu, .gov) and ccTLD (country code: .ca, .co.uk, .ma).
Over 2,000 gTLDs exist today including .online, .club, .website.
Second-Level Domain
The name you register and own “google” in google.com. Max 63 characters, only a-z, 0–9, hyphens.
Subdomain
Sits left of the second-level domain “admin” in admin.google.com. You can chain multiple: jupiter.servers.google.com. Total length under 253 characters, unlimited subdomains allowed.

DNS Record Types
Press enter or click to view image in full size

DNS record typesMX priority: lower number = higher priority. Email tries the highest-priority server first, falling back if it’s down.
TXT uses: proving domain ownership, anti-spam policies (SPF/DMARC), and third-party service verification.

How a DNS Request Actually Works
Press enter or click to view image in full size

How DNS resolution works step-by-stepTTL (Time To Live): every DNS record has a TTL in seconds — how long to cache the answer before re-checking. A TTL of 3600 = cached for 1 hour. This is why the web feels fast.

Part 1 SummaryWhat’s Next?
Now you understand how your browser finds a website using DNS from domain names and DNS records to the chain of servers that translate a human-friendly name into a real IP address.
But finding a website is only the first step.
Once your browser knows where the website lives…
How does it actually communicate with the server?
How does a browser ask for a web page?
And how does the server know what to send back?
That’s where HTTP and HTTPS come in. the languages that power the modern web.
We’ll break down URLs, requests, responses, HTTP methods, status codes, and the secure connection that protects your data online.
👉 Part 2: HTTP & HTTPS How Your Browser Talks to Websites
